name: Understanding Time Complexity (Big O)
chatResponse: I will show you how algorithms scale as data grows, comparing common time complexities from Constant to Quadratic time.

steps.title: Step 1: The Concept of Big O
steps.content: Time complexity measures how the runtime of an algorithm grows relative to the input size ($n$). It's not about seconds, but about the **number of operations** required. We use **Big O Notation** to describe the upper bound (worst-case scenario).

steps.title: Step 2: Excellent Scalability - O(1) and O(log n)
steps.content:
- **O(1) - Constant Time:** Runtime remains the same regardless of input size (e.g., accessing an array element by index).
- **O(log n) - Logarithmic Time:** The problem size is halved each step (e.g., Binary Search). It is incredibly fast even for billions of elements.

steps.title: Step 3: Fair Scalability - O(n) and O(n log n)
steps.content:
- **O(n) - Linear Time:** Performance decreases proportionally to the input (e.g., a single loop through an array).
- **O(n log n) - Linearithmic Time:** Typical for efficient sorting algorithms like Merge Sort or Quick Sort.

steps.title: Step 4: Poor Scalability - O(n²)
steps.content: - **O(n²) - Quadratic Time:** Performance is the square of the input size. This often occurs with nested loops (e.g., Bubble Sort). As $n$ grows, these algorithms become extremely slow and impractical for large datasets.
